title: Queueing Theory
keyQuestion: How do we design service systems to balance capacity costs with customer waiting times?
caseName: Hanover Post Office & Starbucks Service Times
overview: Queueing theory quantifies the relationship between utilization, variability, and waiting times in service systems. The Pollaczek-Khinchine formula reveals why emergency services run at low utilization (for responsiveness) while McDonald's runs at high utilization (for efficiency). Two key insights: waiting times explode exponentially as utilization approaches 100%, and variability in arrivals or service magnifies these effects dramatically. Understanding these mathematical relationships helps you design systems that balance cost (server capacity) with service (customer wait times).

coreContent.title: Basic Queueing Model
coreContent.content: Modeling a Simple Queue: Average arrival rate (Units/Time) = 1/A = λ, Average service rate (Units/Time) = 1/M = μ, where A = Average time between arrivals, M = Average service time. Utilization ρ = M/A = λ/μ = time busy / time available = flow rate / capacity. Assume arrival rate < service rate, else the queue explodes. Little's Law applies: L = λW (customers in queue), WIP = λ(W+M) (total customers in system).
coreContent.keyInsight: Queueing systems must have arrival rate less than service rate, otherwise queues grow without bound and the system becomes unstable.

coreContent.title: Pollaczek-Khinchine Formula (Single Server)
coreContent.content: A useful approximation for the average time W that a customer spends waiting in line (not including service time) is: W = M × [ρ/(1-ρ)] × [(Ca² + Cs²)/2], where Ca = coefficient of variation of time between arrivals = sa/A, Cs = coefficient of variation of service times = ss/M, ρ = utilization = M/A. The average total throughput time = (Wait + Service) = W + M. The formula has two key components: Utilization Effect [ρ/(1-ρ)] and Variance Effect [(Ca² + Cs²)/2].
coreContent.keyInsight: Waiting time increases dramatically as utilization gets very high. Variability in either arrivals or service times magnifies the effects of utilization on waiting times.

coreContent.title: Utilization Effect
coreContent.content: The utilization effect ρ/(1-ρ) shows exponential growth in waiting as utilization approaches 100%. Examples: At ρ = 0.5 (50% utilization): effect = 0.5/0.5 = 1, At ρ = 0.9 (90% utilization): effect = 0.9/0.1 = 9, At ρ = 0.95 (95% utilization): effect = 0.95/0.05 = 19. This explains why emergency services intentionally run at low utilization for responsiveness, while cost-focused operations like McDonald's run at higher utilization for efficiency.
coreContent.keyInsight: The utilization effect creates a 'hockey stick' curve - small increases in utilization near 100% cause dramatic increases in waiting time.

coreContent.title: Variance Effect
coreContent.content: The variance effect (Ca² + Cs²)/2 shows that unpredictability makes everything worse. Ca = σa/A (coefficient of variation for arrivals), Cs = σs/M (coefficient of variation for service). If customers arrive randomly (high Ca) or service times vary a lot (high Cs), waiting times increase. Management actions: Appointments reduce Ca (arrival variability), Training reduces Cs (service variability). A system with no variability (Ca = Cs = 0) has no waiting at all - only the utilization effect matters.
coreContent.keyInsight: Variability always makes waiting worse. Both arrival and service variability matter. Coefficient of variation standardizes variability measurement across different systems.

coreContent.title: Multi-Server Queueing
coreContent.content: For n servers, the waiting time formula becomes: W = (M/n) × [ρ^(√(2(n+1))-1)/(1-ρ)] × [(Ca² + Cs²)/2]. Example: Online retailer help desk with customers arriving every 2 minutes (A=2, sa=2), 3 employees (n=3), service time 4 minutes (M=4, ss=2). Ca = 2/2 = 1, Cs = 2/4 = 0.5, ρ = 4/2 = 2, but per server ρ = 2/3 = 0.67. This gives W ≈ 1.5 minutes wait time.
coreContent.keyInsight: Adding servers provides more than proportional improvement due to pooling effect that reduces overall variability impact. Must consider cost of additional servers vs. waiting cost reduction.

coreContent.title: Psychology of Waiting
coreContent.content: Eight principles for managing customer perception of wait times: 1) Unoccupied time feels longer than occupied time (Action: mirrors by elevators, menus while waiting), 2) Pre-process waits feel longer than in-process waits (Action: triage systems, greeting customers), 3) Anxiety makes waits seem longer (Action: explain delays), 4) Uncertain waits feel longer than known waits (Action: post expected wait times), 5) Unexplained waits feel longer than explained waits (Action: announce reasons for delays), 6) Unfair waits feel longer than equitable waits (Action: number systems), 7) The more valuable the service, the longer customers will wait (Action: express lanes), 8) Solo waits feel longer than group waits.
coreContent.keyInsight: Customer perceptions can be managed - how long people think they waited matters more than actual time in many service contexts.

coreContent.title: Operations Triangle
coreContent.content: The Operations Triangle shows you can't have everything. Tradeoff between Cost, Delivery, Flexibility — and 'bad stuff' (queues, WIP, wait times) sits in the middle as the price you pay. Emphasizing Cost and/or Flexibility means Sacrificing Delivery (longer waits). Emphasizing Delivery and/or Cost means Sacrificing Flexibility (limited options). Emphasizing Delivery and/or Flexibility means Sacrificing Cost (more expensive). Southwest Airlines chose fast delivery and low cost, accepting limited flexibility.
coreContent.keyInsight: Cannot optimize all three dimensions simultaneously. Must choose two priorities and accept constraints on the third. Bad stuff (queues, inventory, waste) is the price of the tradeoff.

formulas.name: Multi-Server Waiting Time
formulas.workedExample: Help desk: 3 servers, M=4 min, A=2 min, so ρ per server = (4/2)/3 = 0.67. With Ca=1, Cs=0.5, gives W ≈ 1.5 minutes

formulas.name: Utilization Formula
formulas.workedExample: If service takes 2 minutes and customers arrive every 3 minutes: ρ = 2/3 = 0.67 or 67% utilization

practiceProblems.question: FAA thumb-print reader analysis: Currently customers arrive every 3 minutes (std dev 3), service takes 2.5 minutes (std dev 2.5). With thumb-print, service becomes 2.75 minutes (std dev 2.512). Calculate average total time with and without thumb-print step.
practiceProblems.solution: Without thumb-print: M=2.5, A=3, ρ=0.833, Ca=1, Cs=1. W = 2.5 × [0.833/0.167] × [2/2] = 12.5 min. Total = 12.5 + 2.5 = 15 min. With thumb-print: M=2.75, ρ=0.917, Cs=0.913. W = 2.75 × [0.917/0.083] × [(1² + 0.913²)/2] = 27.7 min. Total = 30.5 min. Major impact from small service time increase!

practiceProblems.question: Bank with 2 systems: ATM (routine transactions, 1 min service, no variability) gets customers every 2 minutes. Human teller (non-routine, 5 min service, 3 min std dev) gets customers every 8 minutes. Calculate waiting times at each.
practiceProblems.solution: ATM: M=1, A=2, ρ=0.5, Ca=0.5, Cs=0 (no variability). W = 1 × [0.5/0.5] × [0.25/2] = 0.125 minutes. Human teller: M=5, A=8, ρ=0.625, Ca=0.5, Cs=0.6. W = 5 × [0.625/0.375] × [(0.5² + 0.6²)/2] = 2.54 minutes. ATM much faster due to no service variability!

practiceProblems.question: Starbucks service goal: Customers arrive 0.25/min (std dev 5.2 min), service takes 2.2 min (std dev 4 min). Calculate current waiting time and total time. If service drops to 2 min, what service std dev achieves 4-min total time?
practiceProblems.solution: Current: A = 1/0.25 = 4 min, M = 2.2 min, ρ = 0.55, Ca = 5.2/4 = 1.3, Cs = 4/2.2 = 1.82. W = 2.2 × [0.55/0.45] × [(1.3² + 1.82²)/2] = 6.7 min. Total = 8.9 min. For 4-min total with M=2: Need W=2. Using trial and error in formula, service std dev ≈ 1.1 minutes achieves this goal.

keyInsights: Three things that hurt you in operations: Setups, Bottlenecks, Uncertainty & Variability
Waiting time increases dramatically as utilization gets very high
Variability in either arrivals or service times magnifies the effects of utilization on waiting times
Increase the number of servers to reduce waiting times
Decrease variability in arrivals (e.g. with an appointment system)
Decrease variability in service times (e.g. by better training, fewer options, specialization)
Adding servers provides more than proportional improvement due to pooling effect
Operations Triangle: Cannot optimize all three dimensions (Cost, Delivery, Flexibility) simultaneously
Unoccupied time feels longer than occupied time
Uncertain waits feel longer than known waits
Unfair waits feel longer than equitable waits
